Election.title: BV2153 — Felsenthal's Absolute Majority paradox — a majority's first choice loses the approval count
Election.description: From Dan S. Felsenthal, 'Review of Paradoxes Afflicting Various Voting Procedures Where One Out of m Candidates (m ≥ 2) Must Be Elected' (University of Haifa / LSE, revised 26 May 2010; Leverhulme Trust 'Voting Power in Practice' workshop, Château du Baffy, Normandy), Appendix A3: the paradoxes afflicting the Approval voting procedure. Example 7. 100 voters — 51×(Amos>Bella>Chad), 48×(Bella>Chad>Amos), 1×(Chad>Bella>Amos). Amos is ranked FIRST by an absolute majority of the voters (51 of 100) and is the Condorcet winner. But if every voter approves their top TWO preferences, the approval totals are Amos 51, Bella 100, Chad 49 — APPROVAL ELECTS BELLA despite Amos's absolute majority of first preferences: the Absolute Majority paradox. The ranked races on the same orderings show the contrast: instant-runoff (IRV) elects Amos immediately (51 is a first-round majority), and Ranked Robin (Copeland) elects Amos as Condorcet winner (beats Bella 51-49, Chad 51-49).

races.title: Felsenthal Ex.7 — Approval (everyone approves their top two)
Felsenthal Ex.7 — IRV (majority favorite wins round one)

logs: Bella has the most approvals and wins the round
Amos wins round with highest number of wins.
